elements.text: When Schedule E is used to calculate qualifying rental income, the lender must add back any listed depreciation, interest, homeowners' association dues, taxes, or insurance expenses to the borrower's cash flow. Non-recurring property expenses may be added back, if documented accordingly.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See General Requirements for Documenting Rental Income (Subject and Non-Subject Property) , Reconciling Partial or No Rental History on Tax Returns (Schedule E Only) and Treatment of the Income (or Loss) for further instructions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When current lease agreements or market rents reported on Form 1007 or Form 1025 are used, the lender must calculate the rental income by multiplying the gross monthly rent(s) by 75%. (This is referred to as "Monthly Market Rent" on the Form 1007.) The remaining 25% of the gross rent will be absorbed by vacancy losses and ongoing maintenance expenses.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When using a lease agreement, the lease agreement amount must be supported by
elements.label: text

elements.text: Form 1007 or Form 1025, as applicable, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: evidence the terms of the lease have gone into effect. Evidence must include a minimum of:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: two months consecutive bank statements or electronic transfers of rental payments for existing lease agreements, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: copies of the security deposit and first full month's rent check with proof of deposit for newly executed agreements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See Treatment of the Income (or Loss) below for further instructions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The treatment and amount of monthly qualifying rental income (described above in Calculating Monthly Qualifying Rental Income (or Loss) ) used in the calculation of the borrower's total debt-to-income ratio - varies depending on whether the borrower occupies the rental property as their principal residence.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the rental income relates to the borrower's principal residence:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The monthly qualifying rental income (as defined above) must be added to the borrower's total monthly income. (The income is not netted against the PITIA of the property.)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The full amount of the mortgage payment (PITIA) must be included in the borrower's total monthly obligations when calculating the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the rental income (or loss) relates to a property other than the borrower's principal residence:
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the monthly qualifying rental income minus the full PITIA is positive, it must be added to the borrower's total monthly income (subject to the limits in Calculating Monthly Qualifying Rental Income (or Loss)).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the monthly qualifying rental income minus PITIA is negative, the monthly net rental loss must be added to the borrower's total monthly obligations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The full PITIA for the rental property is factored into the amount of the net rental income (or loss); therefore, it should not be counted as a monthly obligation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The full monthly payment for the borrower's principal residence (full PITIA or monthly rent) must be counted as a monthly obligation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: When a borrower owns multiple rental properties, the rental income for all non-subject properties is first calculated for each property, then aggregated. The aggregate total of the income (or loss) is then added to the borrower's total monthly income or included in their monthly obligations, as applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Offsetting Monthly Obligations for Rental Property Reported through a Partnership or an S Corporation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If gross rents and related expenses are reported through a partnership or S corporation, then any rental income (or loss) must be evaluated as self-employment income regardless of the borrower's percentage of ownership interest or whether the borrower is personally obligated on the mortgage debt. However, if the related property is reported on the most recent federal business tax return and it's clear the business is responsible for the payment, the full PITIA can be excluded from the DTI calculation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When rental property is reported through a partnership or an S corporation, but the borrower is personally obligated on the mortgage, the property must be included in the count of financed properties and is subject to the requirements for multiple financed properties as indicated in B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See also B3-3.7-01, Analyzing Partnership Returns for a Partnership or LLC and B3-3.7-02, Analyzing Returns for an S Corporation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae publishes worksheets that lenders may use to calculate rental income. The Income Calculator may also be used to calculate certain rental income. Use of Income Calculator or these worksheets is optional. The worksheets are:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Rental Income Worksheet - Principal Residence, 2- to 4-unit Property (Form 1037),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rental Income Worksheet - Individual Rental Income from Investment Property(s) (up to 4 properties) (Form 1038),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Rental Income Worksheet - Individual Rental Income from Investment Property(s) (up to 10 properties) (Form 1038A).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See B3-3.1-03, Income Calculator, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible rents on the subject property (gross monthly rent) must be reported to Fannie Mae in the loan delivery data for all two- to four-unit principal residence properties and investment properties, regardless of whether the borrower is using rental income to qualify for the loan. If the borrower is using rental income from the subject property to qualify for the loan, all of the applicable requirements above must be followed to document and calculate the income.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower is not using any rental income from the subject property to qualify, gross monthly rent must be documented only for lender reporting purposes. The borrower can provide one of the sources listed above, or may provide one of the following sources (listed in order of preference):
elements.label: text

elements.text: the appraisal report for a one-unit investment property or two- to four-unit property, or Single-Family Comparable Rent Schedule (Form 1007), provided neither the applicable appraisal nor Form 1007 is dated 12 months or more prior to the date of the note;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the property is not currently rented, the lender may use the opinion of market rents provided by the appraiser; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if an appraisal or Form 1007 is not required for the transaction, the lender may rely upon either a signed lease from the borrower or may obtain a statement from the borrower of the gross monthly rent being charged (or to be charged) for the property. The monthly rental amounts must be stated separately for each unit in a two- to four-unit property. The disclosure from the borrower must be in the form of one of the following:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a written statement from the borrower, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must retain the documentation in the loan file that was relied upon to determine the amount of eligible rent reported.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculation of Reserves for Multiple Financed Properties
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Simultaneous Second Home or Investment Property Transactions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Liquid financial reserves are those liquid or near liquid assets that are available to a borrower after the mortgage closes. Liquid financial reserves include cash and other assets that are easily converted to cash by the borrower by
elements.label: text

elements.text: drafting or withdrawing funds from an account,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: obtaining a loan secured by assets from a fund administrator or an insurance company.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reserves are measured by the number of months of the qualifying payment amount for the subject mortgage (based on PITIA) that a borrower could pay using their financial assets. For monthly housing expense and qualifying payment requirements, see B3-6-03, Monthly Housing Expense for the Subject Property and B3-6-04, Qualifying Payment Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The definition of reserves applies to both manually underwritten mortgage loans and loan casefiles underwritten through DU. Funds to close are subtracted from available assets when considering sufficient assets for reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples of liquid financial assets that can be used for reserves include readily available funds in
elements.label: text

elements.text: investments in stocks, bonds, mutual funds, certificates of deposit, money market funds, and trust accounts;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the amount vested in a retirement savings account; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the cash value of a vested life insurance policy.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following cannot be counted as part of the borrower's reserves:
elements.label: text

elements.text: funds that have not been vested;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: funds that cannot be withdrawn under circumstances other than the account owner's retirement, employment termination, or death;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: stock held in an unlisted corporation;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: non-vested stock options and non-vested restricted stock;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: interested party contributions (IPCs) (see B3-4.1-02, Interested Party Contributions (IPCs));
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any amount of a lender contribution (see B3-4.3-06, Grants and Lender Contributions); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: cash proceeds from a cash-out refinance transaction on the subject property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Funds received from acceptable sources may be used to supplement the borrower's funds to satisfy any financial reserve requirement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Eligible gift funds (but not gifts of equity) may be used to satisfy reserve requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Minimum required reserves vary depending on
elements.label: text

elements.text: the occupancy status and amortization type of the subject property,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the number of units in the subject property, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the number of other financed properties the borrower currently owns.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Manually underwritten loans : The minimum required reserves are documented in the Eligibility Matrix .
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU loan casefiles : DU will determine the reserve requirements based on the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Two months' reserves for a second home transaction.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Six months' reserves for the following:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a two- to four-unit principal residence transaction,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a cash-out refinance transaction with a DTI ratio greater than 45%.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional reserves are required when a borrower has multiple financed properties and the subject loan is secured by a second home or investment property. See Calculation of Reserves for Multiple Financed Properties below for additional details.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reserves equal to the balance of 30-day accounts (reduced by the cash back received on a refinance transaction).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional reserves may need to be verified based on DU's overall risk assessment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : There is no minimum reserve requirement for one-unit principal residence transactions. High LTV refinance loans are exempt from the minimum reserve requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculation of Reserves for Multiple Financed Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the borrower owns other financed properties (determined in accordance with B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower), additional reserves must be calculated and documented for financed properties other than the subject property and the borrower's principal residence. The other financed properties reserves amount must be determined by applying a specific percentage to the aggregate of the outstanding unpaid principal balance (UPB) for mortgages and HELOCs on these other financed properties. The percentages are based on the number of financed properties:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 2% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has one to four financed properties,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 4% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has five to six financed properties, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 6% of the aggregate UPB if the borrower has seven to ten financed properties (DU only).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The aggregate UPB calculation does not include the mortgages and HELOCs that are on
elements.label: text

elements.text: properties that are sold or pending sale, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: accounts that will be paid by closing (or omitted in DU on the online loan application).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Simultaneous Second Home or Investment Property Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a lender is processing multiple second home or investment property applications simultaneously, the same assets may be used to satisfy the reserve requirements for both mortgage applications. Reserves are not cumulative for multiple applications.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Example: A lender is simultaneously processing two refinance applications for two investment properties owned by the borrower. The application for property A requires reserves of $5,000. The application for property B requires reserves of $10,000. Because the reserves are covering the same properties, the lender does not have to verify $15,000 in reserves, but only those required per each application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains information on interested party contributions, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Interested party contributions (IPCs) are contributions made by third parties with a vested interest in the transaction. These funds are used to cover costs that are typically the buyer's responsibility. IPCs may include financing or sales concessions, as described below. Fannie Mae does not permit IPCs to be used to make the borrower's down payment, meet financial reserve requirements, or meet minimum borrower contribution requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae considers the following to be IPCs:
elements.label: text

elements.text: funds paid directly by an interested party to the borrower;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: funds that flow through a third-party organization, including nonprofit entities, from the interested party to the borrower;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: funds provided to the transaction on the borrower's behalf by an interested party, including a third-party organization or nonprofit agency; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: funds donated by an interested party to a third party, which then pays some or all of the closing costs for a specific transaction.
elements.label: list_item
